narrative:
## State of the Story: OpenAI Pentagon Contract Controversy

### Key Facts Established

OpenAI announced a deal with the U.S. Department of Defense on Friday, February 28, 2026, to supply AI technology for classified military operations [1][4][5][6]. This contract came after the Pentagon blacklisted Anthropic for refusing to remove contractual restrictions against using its AI for mass domestic surveillance and fully autonomous weapons [1][2][3][4].

Following significant backlash, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman announced on Monday, March 3, that the company would revise its Pentagon agreement [1][4]. Altman acknowledged the deal was "rushed" and "looked opportunistic and sloppy" [1][4][5].

### Timeline of Events

- **Friday, February 28**: Pentagon blacklists Anthropic; OpenAI announces Pentagon deal within hours [1][2]
- **Saturday evening**: Altman holds public Q&A on X defending the decision [5]
- **Monday, March 3**: OpenAI announces contract revisions with new domestic surveillance restrictions [1][4]

### Public and Employee Response

The announcement triggered substantial user backlash against OpenAI. Data shows ChatGPT uninstalls increased by 200% compared to normal rates, while Anthropic's Claude app rose to #1 on the U.S. App Store [1][4]. More than 900 employees from OpenAI and Google signed an open letter opposing their companies' cooperation with Pentagon demands for "domestic mass surveillance and autonomously killing people without human oversight" [1].

### Contract Details and Revisions

The revised OpenAI contract now explicitly states that "the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals" [1]. Additionally, intelligence agencies like the NSA cannot use OpenAI's tools without contract modifications [1][4]. However, legal experts note this approach differs significantly from Anthropic's strategy [6].

### Key Disagreement: Legal vs. Contractual Approach

A fundamental difference emerged between the companies' approaches. Anthropic insisted on "specific prohibitions in the contract," while OpenAI felt "comfortable with" citing applicable laws [6]. Legal expert Jessica Tillipman observed that OpenAI's published contract excerpt "does not give OpenAI an Anthropic-style, free-standing right to prohibit otherwise-lawful government use" [6].

### Ongoing Military AI Usage

Despite the controversy, military AI deployment continues. The BBC reported that Claude was still being used in "the US-Israel war with Iran" as of Tuesday, even after Anthropic's blacklisting [4]. The Pentagon has declined to comment on its dealings with Anthropic [4].

### Market and Strategic Implications

Analysts suggest this controversy may benefit both companies strategically. Anthropic positions itself as "the moral and trustworthy AI provider," which has "market value for both consumers and enterprise clients" [3]. Meanwhile, OpenAI's association with controversial military contracts "could be a trap" for the company [3].

citations.title: OpenAI backtracks on new Pentagon contract, says it will revise deal
citations.quote: We shouldn't have rushed to get this out on Friday. The issues are super complex, and demand clear communication. We were genuinely trying to de-escalate things and avoid a much worse outcome, but I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy

citations.title: The one question everyone should be asking after OpenAI's deal with the Pentagon
citations.quote: When Anthropic didn't back down on its requirements, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth followed through on the latter threat — an unprecedented move, given that the designation has previously been reserved for foreign adversaries like China's Huawei, not American companies

citations.title: Don't bet that the Pentagon – or Anthropic – is acting in the public interest
citations.quote: Posturing publicly against the Pentagon and as a hero to civil libertarians is quite possibly worth the cost of the lost contracts to Anthropic, and associating themselves with the same contracts could be a trap for OpenAI

citations.title: OpenAI changes deal with US military after backlash
citations.quote: According to data from Sensor Tower, the number of people uninstalling ChatGPT has surged since the news of OpenAI's partnership with the Department of Defense was announced on Friday. The market intelligence firm said the daily average uninstall rate was up by 200% compared to normal rates

citations.title: No one has a good plan for how AI companies should work with the government
citations.quote: Around 7 p.m., the OpenAI CEO announced he would be fielding questions publicly on X, as a way of demystifying his company's decision to pick up the Pentagon contract that Anthropic had just walked away from

citations.title: OpenAI's "compromise" with the Pentagon is what Anthropic feared
citations.quote: The published excerpt 'does not give OpenAI an Anthropic-style, free-standing right to prohibit otherwise-lawful government use,' wrote Jessica Tillipman, associate dean for government procurement law studies at George Washington University's law school

keyDevelopments: OpenAI announces Pentagon AI contract after Anthropic blacklisting
Significant user backlash leads to 200% increase in ChatGPT uninstalls
Anthropic's Claude app rises to #1 on App Store
900+ employees from OpenAI and Google sign protest letter
OpenAI revises contract to add domestic surveillance restrictions
Legal experts highlight differences between OpenAI and Anthropic approaches
Claude reportedly still in use for military operations despite blacklisting
